questions.prompt: Why does the chapter describe 'val name = user!!.profile!!.displayName!!' as reintroducing a bug class Kotlin was designed to eliminate, rather than just calling it verbose?

options.text: Each !! is a manual assertion that reintroduces NullPointerException risk at exactly that point — chained together, this opts large parts of the expression back into Java's null-handling risk profile while still paying Kotlin's type-annotation overhead, the worst of both worlds.
The only issue is that this line is longer to type than necessary; there is no correctness difference between the two versions.
!! and ?. are functionally identical in every way; the chapter's distinction is a matter of style preference only.
This pattern is always a compile error in modern Kotlin versions, making the comparison purely historical.

questions.explanation: This is a direct callback to chapter 1's core distinction: each !! is a genuine crash risk, not a stylistic verbosity issue — chaining several compounds the risk at multiple points, while the safe-call-plus-Elvis version eliminates the crash risk entirely with one explicit fallback. (b) trivializes a real correctness/safety difference as mere line length. (c) directly contradicts chapter 1's explicit, detailed distinction between these two operators' behavior. (d) is false; this code compiles fine in current Kotlin — the issue is a design/safety choice, not a compiler restriction.

questions.prompt: What is the chapter's precise criterion for when lateinit is genuinely appropriate, versus when it's papering over a design problem?

options.text: Appropriate when the value genuinely isn't available at construction time (a DI framework or lifecycle callback populates it later); an anti-pattern when the value IS available at construction but lateinit is used just to avoid a longer constructor parameter list.
lateinit should always be preferred over constructor parameters for any property, regardless of when the value becomes available.
lateinit is appropriate only for properties of primitive types like Int or Boolean, never for object references.
The criterion is purely about property visibility; lateinit is fine for public properties but never for private ones.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's precise, stated distinction — the deciding factor is WHEN the value becomes available, not a general preference or convenience shortcut. (b) is exactly the anti-pattern the chapter warns against — using lateinit reflexively rather than for its genuine, narrow use case. (c) is factually backwards; lateinit specifically cannot be used with primitive types in Kotlin (it requires a nullable-reference-compatible type) — it's used for object references, not primitives. (d) fabricates an unrelated visibility-based rule with no basis in the chapter's actual criterion.

questions.prompt: What question does the chapter recommend asking to decide whether a type should be a data class, rather than 'does it need a constructor with some fields'?

options.text: 'Is this a value?' — meaning: are two instances interchangeable if their content matches? Using data class for identity-based entities (compared by ID, not content) is the same category error chapter 2 named, not a style choice.
'Does this class have more than three properties?' — a purely quantitative threshold for choosing data class.
'Will this class ever need to be serialized to JSON?' — data class should be reserved exclusively for serializable types.
There is no meaningful criterion; data class should be the default choice for every class in a Kotlin codebase.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's precise restatement of chapter 2's value-vs-entity distinction, framed as a practical idiom check: content-interchangeability is the actual test, not superficial properties like field count. (b) fabricates an arbitrary numeric threshold unrelated to the actual semantic question. (c) fabricates an unrelated serialization criterion; data class's relevance is about equality/copy semantics, not JSON serializability specifically. (d) directly contradicts chapter 2's explicit warning against using data class for identity-based entities — it is emphatically not a universal default.

questions.prompt: Why does the chapter call this companion object usage an anti-pattern, given that it compiles and works correctly?

options.text: Kotlin allows functions to exist outside any class entirely, so a companion object used purely as a namespace (with no factory-method or privileged-constructor-access need) is importing a Java habit (simulating 'static' methods) into a language that has a more direct, simpler idiom available: a top-level function.
This code fails to compile; companion objects cannot contain functions, only properties.
There is no meaningful difference; the chapter's preference for top-level functions here is purely arbitrary with no stated rationale.
companion object usage is always wrong in Kotlin and should never appear in any codebase under any circumstances.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's precise diagnosis: the anti-pattern isn't companion objects generally, it's using one where NOTHING about it actually needs class association — Kotlin's top-level function support removes the reason Java developers reach for 'static-like' containers out of habit. (b) is false; companion objects can and do commonly contain functions — that's a normal, valid use. (c) understates the chapter's explicit, stated rationale (no genuine need for class association in this specific case). (d) overstates the chapter's position — it explicitly names legitimate companion object uses (factory methods needing constructor access, interface implementation), not a blanket prohibition.

questions.prompt: What is the precise, mechanistic reason a sealed result type is described as 'compiler-enforced' for error handling, while an exception is 'invisible in a function's signature'?

options.text: A function's throws behavior isn't part of Kotlin's type system (unlike Java's checked exceptions) — nothing forces a caller to handle or even know about a possible exception without reading documentation/implementation; a sealed return type makes failure a real, visible part of the signature, and exhaustive when (chapter 3) forces every caller to address every case at compile time.
Kotlin exceptions are actually part of the type system and are just as visible/enforced as sealed result types; the chapter's distinction is inaccurate.
Sealed result types eliminate the possibility of runtime errors entirely, making them strictly superior with no trade-off.
This distinction only applies to checked exceptions, which Kotlin does not have at all, making the entire comparison moot.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's precise mechanism, directly tying back to chapter 3's exhaustive-when guarantee: a return type is checked and enforced by the compiler at every call site, while Kotlin (having no checked exceptions) gives the compiler nothing to enforce about which exceptions a function might throw. (b) directly contradicts the chapter's explicit point — and Kotlin, notably, doesn't even have Java-style checked exceptions, making exception visibility purely a documentation/convention matter. (c) overclaims; sealed result types make EXPECTED failure visible and handled, they don't eliminate all possible runtime errors (a truly unexpected crash can still occur). (d) misses the actual point — the comparison is meaningful precisely BECAUSE Kotlin lacks checked exceptions, making unchecked exceptions genuinely invisible to the type system, which is exactly the gap sealed result types fill.

questions.prompt: A team converts every single function in a small, one-off internal script to return sealed Result types instead of throwing exceptions, reasoning 'this is always safer.' Per the chapter's trade-off, is this the right default?

options.text: Not necessarily — the chapter's explicit criterion is whether CALLERS NEED to be forced to handle the failure case; a quick internal script with a small number of callers doesn't need the ceremony of defining and exhaustively handling sealed cases everywhere, and exceptions remain simpler and appropriate for low-stakes, small-scope code.
Yes, always — sealed result types should replace exceptions universally in all Kotlin code regardless of scope or stakes.
This choice has no practical trade-off either way; the two approaches are functionally and ergonomically identical in all contexts.
Exceptions should never be used in Kotlin under any circumstances, including for genuinely unexpected, unrecoverable errors.

questions.explanation: This is a direct application of the chapter's explicit trade-off statement: the deciding factor is whether callers genuinely need to be forced to handle the failure path, not a blanket 'safer is always better' rule — small-scope, low-stakes code doesn't need the ceremony sealed types add. (b) is exactly the uniform-application mistake the chapter's trade-off paragraph warns against. (c) ignores the chapter's explicit statement that sealed result types add 'real ceremony' exceptions don't require. (d) overstates the chapter's position — it explicitly preserves exceptions as 'the right tool for genuinely exceptional, unrecoverable, or programming-error conditions,' not eliminating them entirely.
